subprinciples.name: Proximity
subprinciples.explanation: Objects close together are perceived as a group.
subprinciples.design_implication: Place related items near each other. Use spacing to signal whether things belong together or are separate.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Equal spacing between all elements, regardless of relationship.

subprinciples.name: Similarity
subprinciples.explanation: Objects that look alike are perceived as related.
subprinciples.design_implication: Use consistent visual treatments for items of the same type.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Styling the same type of content differently across a document.

subprinciples.name: Closure
subprinciples.explanation: The brain fills in missing information to perceive complete shapes.
subprinciples.design_implication: You do not need to draw every border, line, or boundary. Partial cues are sufficient.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Over-specifying every boundary when partial cues would work.

subprinciples.name: Continuity
subprinciples.explanation: The eye follows smooth paths rather than abrupt changes in direction.
subprinciples.design_implication: Align elements along clear axes. Use consistent margins and grid lines.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Misaligned elements that force the eye to zigzag.

subprinciples.name: Figure-Ground
subprinciples.explanation: The brain separates what is important (figure) from what is background (ground).
subprinciples.design_implication: Primary content must be visually distinct from secondary content.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Everything at the same visual level.

subprinciples.name: Common Region
subprinciples.explanation: Objects within a shared boundary are perceived as a group.
subprinciples.design_implication: Cards, containers, and bounded sections group related content.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Related content without visual boundaries when proximity alone is insufficient.

subprinciples.name: Miller's Law
subprinciples.explanation: The average person can hold 7±2 chunks in short-term memory.
subprinciples.design_implication: Break information into groups of 4-6 items.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Presenting more than 7 items without structure.

subprinciples.name: Cognitive Chunking
subprinciples.explanation: People naturally group information into chunks to manage complexity.
subprinciples.design_implication: Design for chunks. Every heading, grouping, and visual boundary is a chunk boundary.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Presenting unstructured lists longer than 6 items.

subprinciples.name: Intrinsic Load
subprinciples.explanation: The inherent complexity of the material. Cannot be reduced, only managed.
subprinciples.design_implication: Present complex content with progressive disclosure, clear hierarchy, and chunking.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Dumping all complex information at once.

subprinciples.name: Extraneous Load
subprinciples.explanation: Cognitive effort wasted on figuring out the interface, layout, or presentation.
subprinciples.design_implication: Eliminate extraneous load through clear, consistent, intentional design.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Decorative elements, inconsistent formatting, unclear hierarchy.

subprinciples.name: Pre-Attentitive Properties
subprinciples.explanation: The brain processes certain visual properties in under 250ms: color, size, orientation, spatial position, contrast.
subprinciples.design_implication: Use pre-attentitive properties to guide attention to the most important information.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Making everything equally prominent.

subprinciples.name: Non-Pre-Attentitive Properties
subprinciples.explanation: Text content, meaning, and relationships require conscious processing.
subprinciples.design_implication: Do not rely on content reading for hierarchy. Use visual properties.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Expecting readers to determine importance by reading all text.

subprinciples.name: F-Pattern
subprinciples.explanation: Readers scan the top, then move down the left side, occasionally darting right.
subprinciples.design_implication: Put the most important information at the top. Use strong left-side headings.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Burying key information in the middle or right of dense text.

subprinciples.name: Z-Pattern
subprinciples.explanation: When there is little text, the eye moves top-left to top-right to bottom-left to bottom-right.
subprinciples.design_implication: Place key information along the Z-axis.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Placing critical information outside the Z path.

subprinciples.name: Gutenberg Diagram
subprinciples.explanation: The page divides into four quadrants. Primary optical area is top-left. Terminal area is bottom-right.
subprinciples.design_implication: Place the most important content top-left and the call-to-action bottom-right.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Placing the primary message in a weak quadrant.

subprinciples.name: Signal vs Noise
subprinciples.explanation: Every element is either signal (essential) or noise (everything else). More noise = harder to extract signal.
subprinciples.design_implication: Remove decorative elements, redundant text, and filler content.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Treating every pixel as equally important.

subprinciples.name: Paradox of Choice
subprinciples.explanation: More options create decision fatigue, not freedom.
subprinciples.design_implication: Fewer choices, presented clearly, lead to better outcomes.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Presenting many options with equal prominence.

subprinciples.name: Layered Understanding
subprinciples.explanation: The brain cannot process all information simultaneously. It builds understanding in layers.
subprinciples.design_implication: Lead with the most important information. Provide detail on demand.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Dumping all information upfront.

subprinciples.name: Predictive Models
subprinciples.explanation: The brain builds predictive models. Violations create surprise, which costs attention.
subprinciples.design_implication: Same content type always looks the same. Same action always appears in the same position.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Inconsistent styling for the same content type.

subprinciples.name: Form Matches Function
subprinciples.explanation: Elements should look like what they are. A button should look clickable.
subprinciples.design_implication: Interactive elements must look interactive. Static elements must not.
subprinciples.anti_pattern: Non-interactive elements styled as interactive (fake buttons, underlined non-links).
